author: @threeMetreJim
text: PC fan is only 2 phase usually. 1 solder spot is the common. You can also tell by how many 'spokes' on the star shaped part. A multiple of three 'spokes' is three phase. A two phase motor will have a multiple of 2 'spokes'. Luckily the PC fan motor has 4 'spokes' and the CD ROM motor has 9 'spokes' so there is no confusion with a number of spokes that is both a multiple of 2 and 3.

replies.author: @vylbird8014
replies.text: It might have something to do with the motor cost. An AC  motor running at the low RPM needed for a ceiling fan needs lots of poles - at least sixteen. That's lots of separate coils, lots of magnets, and lots of physical space to fill with material of suitable magnetic properties. It may well be cheaper to use a simpler-construction and more compact BLDC motor, even after considering the cost of the driver electronics and power supply.

author: @darkobul1
text: Why it called DC when it uses alternating currents?

replies.author: @erlendse
replies.text:
Input to the whole assembly is DC, while it internally use various signals.

DC in, rotation out if you see the whole motor+driver as a block.

replies.author: @darkobul1
replies.text:
@erlendse it DC was real input then you would not need to change currents. Those motors would work on pure sine wave with off phase. Their output is also sine wave.
Those ESC just create varying currents from DC.

replies.author: @erlendse
replies.text:
@darkobul1 1. there are no sines from the driver, it actually outputs square wave.

2. No DC motor can have continous motion without some sort of switching.

brushed DC motors use brushes to create AC for the coils in the rotor.

The driver(often called ESC) does the job brushes would do on a brushed motor.

replies.author: @darkobul1
replies.text: @erlendse i know but that is change of current. That square wave gets damped by coils in motor and mare change of current from VCC to ground are also AC. But thank you for trying to explain it. Its very confusing.

replies.author: @vylbird8014
replies.text: The motor itsself is AC, but for practical purposes it is considered as a unit together with the electronics that drive it. In larger motors they even come integrated in one case. The electronics run off of DC.

author: @kreynolds1123
text:
While Delta wired motors is always 3 wires, 3 wires may be either Delta or it may be a Wye without a neutral wire, and 4 wires is a Wye with a neutral wire, but most 3 phase Wye motors do not need a neutral as current is never conducted on the neutral. As for solder points, a 4th solder point not connected to anything may very likely be neutral of a wye and just 3 solder points may still be a wye where the neutral is not soldered to the board. 

With Wye configuration you always energize 2 coils at a time. With Delta configuration you only energize one coil at a time.

replies.author: @erlendse
replies.text:
Most drivers use the senter for sensing and drive two windings, if center is used at all.

With Wye two coils would be powered, while delta it would effectively be all 3 with different ratios.

replies.author: @kreynolds1123
replies.text:
@erlendse your mostly right on the delta coil energized, and I was just wrong.  With Delta, most of the time all three coils are energized, but there are moments (instances) when anyone of the coils have 0 current, as the current moves to reverse direction. 

Are you sure that wye neutral is used for sensing?  My axial flux BLDC motor on my channel is wired wye and neutral is not used.

replies.author: @erlendse
replies.text:
@kreynolds1123 The sensing part is true for some of the 6 step BLDC drivers.
Not used on motors with sensors, or vector control.

Many actually don't use the center wire or simulate it with 3 resistors.

The drivers that use it have 4 connections for the motor.

replies.author: @kreynolds1123
replies.text:
@erlendse ahhhhhh, I see. For back emf based electronic speed controllers, three resistors in wye create a virtual neutral to compare against and detect the zero crossing. 

I see that a virtual neutral network or resistor can work with both Delta and Wye motors.  But, without a virtual neutral one needs a Wye motor with a real neutral wire as a Delta can not provide a neutral reference without a virtual neutral.

replies.author: @erlendse
replies.text: @kreynolds1123 Yes, that's basically it.

author: @RogerJonker
text: Great video!. I swear on coreless motors as generators. They don't have magnetic cogging and so are very efficient.

author: @totherarf
text:
Nice video!
Back in the mists of time (it really was a long time ago) we were taught "Star" and "Delta"! 
If you reverse any 2 of the 3 phase wires you reverse the direction of the motor (handy if you need a reverse switch)

If you go big scale you get a Star Delta transformer in your substation. The HV in pylons is Delta and the 240/425v supply is star with your neutral off the center .... and the earth too!
